one_line_takeaway: A stability-based, end-to-end theory proving RLHF under a linear reward model attains a dimension-free O~(n^-1/2) suboptimality gap, recoverable by GA (T=n^2) and SGA (T=n^4), under a novel feature-coverage assumption.
key_finding: Under the feature-coverage assumption, RLHF's suboptimality gap is dimension-free O~(n^-1/2) for empirical optima, and the same order is recoverable for gradient-based training by running enough steps: GA needs T=n^2 and SGA needs T=n^4. Notably, contrary to the classic Hardt et al. (2016) intuition that more non-convex iterations worsen stability, here algorithmic stability DECREASES with T, because Lemma 6 ties stability to the (shrinking) empirical gradient norm of the returned iterate. In the sufficient-coverage regime (Theorem 1), the empirical stationary point exactly equals the ground-truth theta* with high probability once n > (128 C^4/sigma^2)(log(1/rho) + d log(432 R C^5/sigma^2)).
core_contribution: The paper builds an end-to-end generalization theory for RLHF of LLMs under a linear reward model, analyzed through the lens of algorithmic (uniform) stability rather than the prevailing MLE-reward-consistency paradigm. Its own contributions are: (1) a decomposition of the policy's suboptimality gap J(pi_theta*) - J(pi_thetaS) into concentration, optimization, and generalization errors (Definition 3); (2) a new 'feature coverage' assumption (Assumption 5) positing that the column space of the empirical feature-covariance matrix V_S progressively covers the feature space as n grows, with residual epsilon_n = O(n^-1); (3) a proof that empirical stationary points of the KL-regularized RLHF objective attain a dimension-free suboptimality gap of order O~(n^-1/2) (Theorems 2-3); and (4) extension of the stability analysis to iterative optimizers, giving explicit suboptimality bounds for Gradient Ascent (GA) O~(T^-1/4 + n^-1/2) and Stochastic Gradient Ascent (SGA) O~(T^-1/8 + n^-1/2) (Theorems 4-7). Cited/prior building blocks it relies on but does NOT claim: the closed-form Boltzmann optimal-policy form (Proposition 1 = Rafailov et al. 2023), the uniform-stability framework (Bousquet & Elisseeff 2002; Bousquet et al. 2020; Hardt et al. 2016), and the MLE-consistency analyses it argues against (Zhu et al. 2023; Xiong et al. 2023).
threat_model: N/A -- this is a theoretical learning-theory paper on the generalization of RLHF. There is no adversary, attack, or defense; no threat model is defined and none should be imputed. Safety relevance (from the paper's own Impact Statement only): dimension-independent bounds suggest alignment can be sample-efficient under sufficient feature coverage, but the authors caution that the linear-reward assumption may underestimate alignment errors under model misspecification in safety-critical domains, and that algorithmic stability implies societal biases in the training data would be faithfully propagated to the deployed policy.
limitations: Strong assumptions limit reach: linear reward realizability (Assumption 2) and the posterior-Boltzmann policy class; access to ground-truth rewards rather than learned-from-preference rewards (deviating from the standard two-stage RLHF pipeline); the feature-coverage assumption with epsilon_n=O(n^-1) residual (Assumption 5) and linear independence of per-prompt action features with |A|<=d^2 (Assumption 4). The SGA guarantee requires selecting the min-gradient-norm iterate (full-gradient evaluation each step), not the last iterate; last-iterate results would need stronger conditions (e.g. PL). Empirical validation is simulation-only with tiny scales (d=24, d=32; n up to ~300; step-scaling shown at n=7 and n=10), so the theory is not tested on real LLMs or real preference data. The authors themselves note the linear-reward assumption may not capture non-linear human preferences or model-misspecification risks.

datasets_used: No real-world datasets: validation is synthetic/simulation only
Simulated linear-reward RLHF problems at dimensions d=24 and d=32 (suboptimality-vs-n experiments)
Simulated problems at sample sizes n up to ~300, and small n=7 / n=10 for the suboptimality-vs-steps-T experiments
Simulations sweeping sigma_min of the expected feature-covariance matrix (values proportional to 0.02-0.2) to verify positive-definiteness / exact-recovery scaling
quantitative_results: Suboptimality gap of empirical stationary points: O~(n^-1/2) (Theorems 2-3), dimension-free
Gradient Ascent suboptimality gap: O~(T^-1/4 + n^-1/2) (Lemma 3, Theorems 4-5); collapses to O~(n^-1/2) when T=n^2
Stochastic Gradient Ascent suboptimality gap: O~(T^-1/8 + n^-1/2) (Lemmas 4-5, Theorems 6-7); collapses to O~(n^-1/2) when T=n^4
Exact-recovery sample complexity (Theorem 1): n > (128 C^4/sigma^2)(log(1/rho) + d log(432 R C^5/sigma^2)) yields theta* as the unique empirical stationary point in Theta_R with probability >= 1-rho
GA convergence (Lemma 2): ||grad J_S(theta_GA)||^2 <= 12 L_f R C / T with step size eta=1/L_f
Failure probability of non-positive-definite covariance scales as rho ~ exp(-n/sigma_min), verified in Figure 2
Parameter search region fixed to R=3D; KL weight set to lambda=1 w.l.o.g.
baselines_compared: Three optimization regimes compared in simulation against a 1/sqrt(n) reference line: Exact Optimization (empirical stationary point), Gradient Ascent, and Stochastic GA (Figure 1)

Conceptual contrast (not numeric) against the prior MLE-reward-consistency analytical framework of Zhu et al. (2023) and Xiong et al. (2023), which the paper argues fails to capture the RL process and needs stringent data-coverage assumptions
No LLM/model baselines: the paper trains no language models and reports no NLP task metrics
